summary: Stancil argues that organizations, like individuals, rely mostly on fast, intuitive “System 1” thinking, so analytics teams are misguided when they try to out-reason it with pure analysis. Instead of chasing isolated actionable insights, analysts should work with System 1 by surfacing and updating the organization’s underlying mental models. The goal is to reshape heuristics and theories that guide many decisions, not just provide one-off recommendations.
money_quotes: No matter how fast we are or how good our tools get, most decisions will be made using System 1; we can’t whack-a-mole them away, by putting an analyst in every room or a BI tool on every laptop.
If we are the company’s second system, we’ll always be second chair.
The much more powerful thing that we could do is teach a company a theory—or as Ahbi put it, our job shouldn’t be to look for insights, but to mutate mental models.
Don’t veto biased thinking; update the bias.
Theories, by contrast, ask people to see the world differently.
key_insight: Analytics teams create more leverage by reshaping an organization’s underlying heuristics than by delivering isolated actionable insights.
